vignette: A 55-year-old male with cirrhosis (Child-Pugh B, MELD 18) presents with hematemesis. Vitals: BP 85/50, HR 120. He is confused. Exam shows ascites and spider angiomata. What is the FIRST step in management?

options.text: IV octreotide + IV ceftriaxone + IV fluids

explanation.whyCorrect: Variceal hemorrhage: 1) IV fluids, 2) IV octreotide, 3) IV ceftriaxone. Then urgent endoscopy within 12 hours.

whyWrong.A: Endoscopy is step 2 (after stabilization).
whyWrong.C: Blakemore tube is rescue therapy.
whyWrong.D: TIPS is for refractory bleeding.
whyWrong.E: PPIs are for PEPTIC ulcer bleeding.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Variceal bleed: Octreotide + Ceftriaxone + Fluids → Urgent EGD (banding).

vignette: A 42-year-old female presents with severe epigastric pain radiating to the back, nausea, and vomiting after a fatty meal. Lipase is 1,200 U/L. CT shows acute interstitial pancreatitis with no necrosis. What is the MOST important next step?

options.text: Start early enteral nutrition within 24 hours

explanation.whyCorrect: Early enteral nutrition (within 24-48 hours) reduces mortality, infections, and length of stay.

whyWrong.A: Prophylactic antibiotics are NOT recommended.
whyWrong.C: NPO and TPN are outdated. Enteral nutrition is superior.
whyWrong.D: ERCP is for gallstone pancreatitis with cholangitis.
whyWrong.E: Surgery is for infected necrosis after 4 weeks.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Pancreatitis: Aggressive IV fluids + Early enteral nutrition = Reduced mortality.

vignette: A 28-year-old female presents with jaundice, fatigue, and arthralgias. Labs: AST 180, ALT 220, ALP 150, Total bilirubin 4.5. ANA positive (1:320), Anti-smooth muscle antibody positive. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?

explanation.whyCorrect: Young female + Hepatitis pattern + ANA + ASMA positive = Autoimmune hepatitis Type 1.

whyWrong.A: PBC has cholestatic pattern + AMA positive.
whyWrong.C: PSC has cholestatic pattern + MRCP shows beaded ducts.
whyWrong.D: Drug-induced would not have positive autoimmune markers.
whyWrong.E: Wilson presents with neuropsychiatric symptoms + KF rings.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Autoimmune hepatitis: Young female + Hepatitis pattern + ANA/ASMA positive. Treatment: Steroids + Azathioprine.

vignette: A 50-year-old male at average risk for colorectal cancer asks about screening options. He prefers a non-invasive test. Which is the MOST appropriate recommendation?

explanation.whyCorrect: CRC screening starts at 45. Non-invasive option: FIT annually. If positive → colonoscopy.

whyWrong.A: Colonoscopy is invasive. Patient requested non-invasive.
whyWrong.C: CT colonography is an option but FIT is preferred.
whyWrong.D: Flex sig is less common now.
whyWrong.E: Screening starts at 45, not 55.

explanation.clinicalPearl: CRC screening: Start at 45! Options: Colonoscopy q10yr, FIT q1yr.

vignette: A 48-year-old male with history of heartburn for 10 years presents with progressive dysphagia to solids. EGD shows distal esophageal stricture and biopsy shows intestinal metaplasia without dysplasia. What is the BEST long-term management?

options.text: Endoscopic surveillance every 3-5 years + PPI BID
Endoscopic surveillance every 1 year + PPI daily

explanation.whyCorrect: Barrett esophagus WITHOUT dysplasia: Surveillance every 3-5 years + PPI BID.

whyWrong.B: EMR is for visible lesions or high-grade dysplasia.
whyWrong.C: Esophagectomy is last resort.
whyWrong.D: Annual surveillance is for low-grade dysplasia.
whyWrong.E: RFA is for dysplasia.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Non-dysplastic Barrett: PPI BID + surveillance q3-5yr.

vignette: A 45-year-old obese female presents with episodic RUQ pain after fatty meals for 3 months. US shows multiple gallstones, CBD 4mm, no wall thickening. LFTs are normal. What is the BEST management?

explanation.whyCorrect: Symptomatic cholelithiasis: Elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy is definitive treatment.

whyWrong.B: ERCP is for choledocholithiasis or cholangitis.
whyWrong.C: UDCA is for non-surgical candidates.
whyWrong.D: Symptomatic gallstones should be treated surgically.
whyWrong.E: Emergency surgery is for acute cholecystitis.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Symptomatic cholelithiasis: Elective cholecystectomy. Acute cholecystitis: Early cholecystectomy (within 72h).

vignette: A 28-year-old male with ulcerative colitis (pancolitis) for 5 years presents with worsening bloody diarrhea, urgency, and weight loss. He has failed mesalamine and azathioprine. Colonoscopy shows severe active colitis. What is the BEST next step?

explanation.whyCorrect: Severe UC flare failing mesalamine + thiopurine: Start steroids (prednisone or IV methylprednisolone) for rapid symptom control + Anti-TNF (infliximab) for long-term remission. Anti-TNFs work faster than vedolizumab.

whyWrong.A: Anti-TNF alone may not be sufficient for severe acute flare.
whyWrong.B: Already failed azathioprine, increasing dose unlikely to help.
whyWrong.C: Vedolizumab has slower onset (6-12 weeks), not ideal for severe flare.
whyWrong.D: Surgery is last resort after failing medical therapy (3 biologics).

explanation.clinicalPearl: Severe UC flare: Steroids (bridge) + Anti-TNF (infliximab). Vedolizumab for maintenance. Surgery if refractory.

vignette: A 45-year-old male presents with jaundice, fatigue, and RUQ pain for 1 week. He ate at a seafood restaurant 4 weeks ago. Labs: AST 1200, ALT 1500, ALP 200, Bilirubin 8.0. Hepatitis serologies: HAV IgM positive. What is the expected outcome?

options.text: Progression to chronic hepatitis in 80% of cases
Acute hepatitis with complete recovery in >99% of cases

explanation.whyCorrect: Acute Hepatitis A: Fecal-oral transmission (contaminated seafood). Self-limited, complete recovery in >99%. NO chronic carrier state. Treatment: Supportive. Prevention: HAV vaccine.

whyWrong.A: HAV does NOT cause chronic hepatitis (unlike HBV and HCV).
whyWrong.C: Fulminant hepatitis is rare (<1%), mostly in elderly or underlying liver disease.
whyWrong.D: HCC risk is with chronic HBV/HCV, not acute HAV.
whyWrong.E: HAV does not progress to cirrhosis.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Hepatitis A: Acute only, NEVER chronic. Fecal-oral spread. Complete recovery in >99%. Vaccine available.

vignette: A 30-year-old female presents with chronic diarrhea, weight loss, and megaloblastic anemia. She has a history of recurrent oral ulcers. Small bowel biopsy shows villous atrophy with increased intraepithelial lymphocytes. Which HLA type is MOST strongly associated?

explanation.whyCorrect: Celiac disease: Villous atrophy + intraepithelial lymphocytosis. HLA-DQ2 (90-95%) or HLA-DQ8 (5-10%) is present in >99% of patients. High negative predictive value (if negative, rules out celiac). Associated with dermatitis herpetiformis.

whyWrong.A: HLA-B27: Ankylosing spondylitis, reactive arthritis, IBD-associated arthritis.
whyWrong.C: HLA-B51: Behcet disease (oral/genital ulcers, uveitis).
whyWrong.D: HLA-DR4: Rheumatoid arthritis.
whyWrong.E: HLA-A3: Hemochromatosis.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Celiac disease: HLA-DQ2/DQ8. Villous atrophy + IEL. Treatment: Strict gluten-free diet. Check tTG-IgA for screening.

vignette: A 70-year-old male presents with painless jaundice, weight loss, and a palpable gallbladder (Courvoisier sign). CT shows a 3cm mass in the head of the pancreas with biliary dilation. CA 19-9 is elevated. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?

explanation.whyCorrect: Pancreatic adenocarcinoma: Painless jaundice + Courvoisier sign (palpable, non-tender gallbladder) + Weight loss + Mass in head of pancreas. Most common pancreatic malignancy. CA 19-9 is tumor marker (not diagnostic). Treatment: Whipple procedure if resectable.

whyWrong.A: Pseudocyst has history of pancreatitis, not painless jaundice.
whyWrong.B: Chronic pancreatitis causes pain, calcifications, not mass with obstruction.
whyWrong.D: PNETs are less common, often functional (insulin, gastrin), and may not cause jaundice.
whyWrong.E: AIP responds to steroids, has IgG4 elevation, and diffuse 'sausage-shaped' pancreas.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Painless jaundice + Courvoisier sign + Pancreatic head mass = Adenocarcinoma until proven otherwise. Only 15-20% resectable at diagnosis.

vignette: A 50-year-old obese diabetic male has elevated AST 70, ALT 90, and echogenic liver on ultrasound. Hepatitis panel is negative. He drinks 1 glass of wine weekly. What is the diagnosis per AASLD 2024 nomenclature?

explanation.whyCorrect: AASLD/EASL 2024: NAFLD renamed to MASLD (Metabolic dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease). Criteria: Hepatic steatosis + ≥1 cardiometabolic risk factor (obesity, diabetes, hypertension, hypertriglyceridemia, low HDL). Term 'fatty' replaced with 'steatotic' to reduce stigma. MASH (Metabolic dysfunction-Associated Steatohepatitis) replaces NASH. Patient has steatosis + obesity + diabetes = MASLD.

whyWrong.A: NAFLD is outdated terminology (replaced by MASLD in 2024).
whyWrong.B: MASLD: New nomenclature emphasizing metabolic dysfunction, not 'non-alcoholic'.
whyWrong.C: Alcohol intake <20g/day (women) or <30g/day (men) rules out alcoholic liver disease.
whyWrong.D: Autoimmune hepatitis: ANA/ASMA positive, IgG elevated, interface hepatitis on biopsy.
whyWrong.E: Hemochromatosis: Elevated ferritin + Transferrin saturation >45%, HFE gene.

explanation.clinicalPearl: NAFLD is now MASLD (2024). NASH is now MASH. Focus on metabolic dysfunction, not alcohol exclusion. Weight loss 7-10% = cornerstone.

vignette: A 45-year-old male with H. pylori-positive duodenal ulcer has failed clarithromycin-based triple therapy. What is the BEST salvage regimen per ACG 2024?

options.text: Bismuth quadruple therapy (PPI + Bismuth + Tetracycline + Metronidazole) x14 days

explanation.whyCorrect: Failed clarithromycin triple therapy (high clarithromycin resistance in US >15%): Bismuth quadruple therapy (BQT) is recommended salvage. PPI + Bismuth + Tetracycline + Metronidazole x14 days. Achieves >90% eradication. Levofloxacin triple therapy is alternative but resistance increasing. Rifabutin triple therapy is another option. Always confirm eradication (urea breath test or stool antigen) 4 weeks after treatment.

whyWrong.A: Repeating clarithromycin after failure is inappropriate (high resistance).
whyWrong.B: Bismuth quadruple therapy is preferred salvage regimen.
whyWrong.C: Levofloxacin triple therapy is alternative but fluoroquinolone resistance increasing.
whyWrong.D: PPI alone does not eradicate H. pylori.
whyWrong.E: Monotherapy is ineffective and promotes resistance.

explanation.clinicalPearl: H. pylori salvage: Bismuth quadruple therapy (BQT) x14 days. Levofloxacin or Rifabutin triple therapy if BQT fails. Confirm eradication!

vignette: A 50-year-old male with acute pancreatitis (Ranson score 3, BUN 28, Hct 48%) presents within 6 hours of pain onset. What is the BEST fluid resuscitation strategy per AGA 2023?

options.text: Lactated Ringer's solution at 3 mL/kg/hr (aggressive) for first 12-24 hours

explanation.whyCorrect: AGA 2023: Moderate-severe pancreatitis (elevated BUN, hemoconcentration): AGGRESSIVE Lactated Ringer's (LR) at 3 mL/kg/hr for first 12-24 hours, then reassess. LR is PREFERRED over NS (less hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis, anti-inflammatory properties). Goal: Decrease BUN and Hct within 12-24 hours (indicates adequate resuscitation). Avoid over-resuscitation after 24-48h.

whyWrong.A: 1.5 mL/kg/hr is maintenance; this patient needs aggressive resuscitation (3 mL/kg/hr).
whyWrong.B: LR 3 mL/kg/hr = Aggressive resuscitation. LR > NS for pancreatitis.
whyWrong.C: Bolus alone insufficient; needs ongoing aggressive rate.
whyWrong.D: Colloids have no proven benefit over crystalloids in pancreatitis.
whyWrong.E: Fluid restriction contraindicated in acute pancreatitis (third-spacing requires aggressive replacement).

explanation.clinicalPearl: Pancreatitis: AGGRESSIVE LR (3 mL/kg/hr x12-24h). LR > NS. Goal: ↓BUN, ↓Hct. Avoid over-resuscitation after 24-48h.

vignette: A 70-year-old female develops C. difficile diarrhea (PCR positive, non-severe per IDSA criteria) after a course of ciprofloxacin for UTI. What is the FIRST-LINE treatment per IDSA 2024?

options.text: Oral vancomycin 125mg PO QID x10 days OR Fidaxomicin 200mg PO BID x10 days

explanation.whyCorrect: IDSA/SHEA 2021 (still current 2024): Oral vancomycin 125mg QID OR Fidaxomicin 200mg BID are FIRST-LINE for initial non-severe C. diff. Metronidazole is NO LONGER first-line (lower efficacy). Fidaxomicin has lower recurrence rate but higher cost. IV vancomycin has NO effect on C. diff (not excreted into colon). FMT for ≥3 recurrences.

whyWrong.A: Metronidazole is second-line (only if vancomycin/fidaxomicin unavailable).
whyWrong.B: Oral vancomycin or Fidaxomicin are first-line. Fidaxomicin preferred if high recurrence risk.
whyWrong.C: IV vancomycin does NOT reach colonic lumen.
whyWrong.D: FMT is for ≥3 recurrences, not initial episode.
whyWrong.E: Probiotics have limited evidence; not recommended as sole therapy.

explanation.clinicalPearl: C. diff (initial, non-severe): Oral vancomycin 125mg QID OR Fidaxomicin 200mg BID. Metronidazole NO LONGER first-line.

vignette: A 55-year-old male with cirrhosis due to MASLD (Child-Pugh A, MELD 10) asks about HCC screening. What is the recommended method per AASLD 2024?

explanation.whyCorrect: AASLD 2024: HCC surveillance in cirrhosis = Ultrasound + AFP every 6 months. US alone has 45-60% sensitivity; adding AFP increases sensitivity to 63-70%. MRI/CT reserved for: Inadequate US (obesity, steatosis), nodules on US, or rising AFP. CT has radiation risk (not ideal for long-term surveillance). Surveillance improves early detection and survival.

whyWrong.A: CT is not first-line for surveillance (radiation, cost).
whyWrong.B: US + AFP q6mo is the standard surveillance strategy.
whyWrong.C: MRI is reserved for inadequate US or high-risk patients (cost, access).
whyWrong.D: US q12mo has lower sensitivity than q6mo.
whyWrong.E: PET-CT not recommended for HCC surveillance.

explanation.clinicalPearl: HCC surveillance in cirrhosis: US + AFP every 6 months. CT/MRI if US inadequate or abnormal findings.

vignette: A 25-year-old male with dysphagia to solids and history of food impaction has esophageal biopsies showing >15 eosinophils/HPF despite PPI trial. What is the BEST treatment per AGA 2024?

explanation.whyCorrect: EoE refractory to PPI: Dupilumab is the FIRST and ONLY FDA-approved medication for EoE (2022). Anti-IL4/IL13 pathway. Improves symptoms and reduces eosinophilic infiltration. Alternative: Topical swallowed steroids (budesonide slurry or fluticasone) or dietary elimination (6-food elimination diet). Dilation for strictures. Systemic steroids avoided due to side effects.

whyWrong.A: Already failed PPI (trial completed).
whyWrong.B: Dupilumab: FDA-approved for EoE. First biologic for EoE.
whyWrong.C: Systemic steroids effective but side effects limit long-term use.
whyWrong.D: Dilation treats strictures, not underlying inflammation.
whyWrong.E: Dietary elimination is effective but challenging to maintain.

explanation.clinicalPearl: EoE refractory to PPI: Dupilumab (FDA-approved 2022). Topical steroids alternative. Dietary elimination is non-pharmacologic option.

vignette: A 60-year-old male with alcoholic cirrhosis (MELD 22, ascites) develops AKI (Cr 2.5 from baseline 1.0) after large-volume paracentesis without albumin. He is not on diuretics. No proteinuria/hematuria. What is the diagnosis per ICA 2024?

explanation.whyCorrect: HRS-AKI (new nomenclature replacing HRS-1): Cirrhosis + Ascites + AKI (Cr increase ≥0.3 within 48h or ≥1.5x baseline) + No response to diuretic withdrawal and albumin challenge (1g/kg x2 days) + No shock, nephrotoxins, proteinuria, or obstruction. Paracentesis without albumin → volume depletion → precipitates HRS. Treatment: Terlipressin + Albumin (FDA-approved 2022) + Liver transplant evaluation.

whyWrong.A: Pre-renal AKI responds to volume expansion; HRS-AKI does not improve after albumin.
whyWrong.B: HRS-AKI: Functional renal failure in cirrhosis. Terlipressin + Albumin = Treatment.
whyWrong.C: ATN: Muddy brown casts, FeNa >2%, history of prolonged hypotension or nephrotoxins.
whyWrong.D: IgA nephropathy: Hematuria, proteinuria, not isolated AKI in cirrhosis.
whyWrong.E: No contrast exposure history.

explanation.clinicalPearl: HRS-AKI: Cirrhosis + Ascites + AKI + No response to albumin. Terlipressin + Albumin (FDA 2022). Liver transplant = definitive.

vignette: A 35-year-old female with moderate-severe UC (Mayo score 8) has failed mesalamine and azathioprine. What is the BEST first-line advanced therapy per AGA 2024?

explanation.whyCorrect: AGA 2024: Upadacitinib (selective JAK1 inhibitor) is now PREFERRED first-line advanced therapy for moderate-severe UC. U-ACHIEVE and U-ACCOMPLISH trials showed superior efficacy vs placebo with rapid onset. Oral administration is advantage over injectables. Higher response rates than vedolizumab and adalimumab in head-to-head trials. Tofacitinib is older JAK inhibitor (more side effects).

whyWrong.A: Anti-TNF is effective but upadacitinib has higher efficacy in recent trials.
whyWrong.B: Upadacitinib: Oral, rapid onset, superior efficacy. Preferred first-line per AGA 2024.
whyWrong.C: Vedolizumab is gut-selective but slower onset and lower efficacy than upadacitinib.
whyWrong.D: Ustekinumab is effective but slower onset than upadacitinib.
whyWrong.E: Tofacitinib is older JAK inhibitor (pan-JAK); upadacitinib is more selective (JAK1).

explanation.clinicalPearl: Moderate-severe UC: Upadacitinib (JAK1 inhibitor) = New first-line advanced therapy. Oral, rapid onset. Superior to vedolizumab.

vignette: A 55-year-old male with long-segment Barrett esophagus (C5M7) has biopsies showing non-dysplastic intestinal metaplasia on 2 consecutive endoscopies. What is the appropriate surveillance interval per ACG 2024?

explanation.whyCorrect: Non-dysplastic Barrett esophagus: Surveillance EGD every 3-5 years. Long-segment (≥3cm) = every 3 years. Short-segment (<3cm) = every 5 years. If low-grade dysplasia: EGD every 6-12 months or consider endoscopic eradication. If high-grade dysplasia: Endoscopic eradication therapy (RFA or EMR). PPI BID to control reflux.

whyWrong.A: Every 3 months is too frequent for non-dysplastic BE.
whyWrong.B: Every 3-5 years is standard for non-dysplastic BE based on segment length.
whyWrong.C: Annual surveillance is for low-grade dysplasia, not non-dysplastic.
whyWrong.D: Endoscopic eradication is for high-grade dysplasia or intramucosal carcinoma.
whyWrong.E: Non-dysplastic BE requires ongoing surveillance (risk of progression 0.3%/year).

explanation.clinicalPearl: Barrett surveillance: Non-dysplastic → q3-5yr. Low-grade dysplasia → q6-12mo or eradication. High-grade dysplasia → Eradication therapy.

vignette: A 45-year-old male with chronic alcoholic pancreatitis has debilitating epigastric pain radiating to the back despite abstinence and pancreatic enzymes. CT shows calcifications and dilated pancreatic duct (7mm). What is the BEST next step?

options.text: ERCP with pancreatic duct stenting or ESWL (lithotripsy) for stones

explanation.whyCorrect: Chronic pancreatitis with dilated duct >5mm and stones: Endoscopic therapy (ERCP with stenting ± ESWL for pancreatic duct stones) is first-line intervention. Provides pain relief by decompressing obstructed duct. If endoscopic therapy fails: Surgical drainage (Puestow/lateral pancreaticojejunostomy). Celiac plexus block provides temporary relief only. Total pancreatectomy is last resort.

whyWrong.A: Escalating opioids risks dependence and opioid-induced hyperalgesia.
whyWrong.B: ERCP + ESWL targets the mechanical cause of pain (ductal obstruction).
whyWrong.C: Total pancreatectomy is last resort (brittle diabetes).
whyWrong.D: Celiac block is temporary (weeks-months) and does not address obstruction.
whyWrong.E: Medical management has failed; mechanical obstruction needs intervention.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Chronic pancreatitis pain + Dilated duct + Stones: ERCP + ESWL first. Surgery if endoscopic therapy fails. Opioids last resort.

vignette: A 30-year-old male with moderate-severe ileocolonic Crohn disease has failed adalimumab (anti-TNF). He has active inflammation on colonoscopy and elevated CRP. What is the BEST next advanced therapy per AGA 2024?

explanation.whyCorrect: Failed anti-TNF: Switch to different MECHANISM of action. Risankizumab (anti-IL23 p19) is FDA-approved for moderate-severe Crohn disease. ADVANCE and MOTIVATE trials showed superior efficacy over placebo. Ustekinumab (anti-IL12/23) is alternative. Switching within same class (infliximab) may work but different mechanism preferred. Vedolizumab is gut-selective but slower onset.

whyWrong.A: Switching within anti-TNF class less effective than switching mechanism.
whyWrong.B: Anti-IL23 (risankizumab) = Different mechanism, high efficacy, favorable safety.
whyWrong.C: Vedolizumab is effective but slower onset; different mechanism is good option.
whyWrong.D: Azathioprine monotherapy is inadequate for moderate-severe disease.
whyWrong.E: Long-term steroids not acceptable (osteoporosis, infection, metabolic side effects).

explanation.clinicalPearl: Crohn after anti-TNF failure: Switch mechanism (anti-IL23 = risankizumab, or anti-IL12/23 = ustekinumab). Avoid long-term steroids.

vignette: A 55-year-old male with uncomplicated left-sided diverticulitis (Hinchey Ia, CT-confirmed, no abscess) presents with moderate pain but tolerating oral intake. No immunosuppression. What is the BEST management per AGA 2023?

options.text: Outpatient management with oral antibiotics (amoxicillin-clavulanate) OR observation without antibiotics

explanation.whyCorrect: AGA 2023: Uncomplicated diverticulitis in immunocompetent patient tolerating oral intake = Outpatient management. Antibiotics may be SELECTIVE (not mandatory) - observation without antibiotics is acceptable for mild cases (AVOD trial). If antibiotics used: Amoxicillin-clavulanate or TMP-SMX + Metronidazole x7-10 days. Colonoscopy in 6-8 weeks to rule out malignancy. Surgery for recurrent or complicated.

whyWrong.A: IV antibiotics reserved for complicated diverticulitis or inability to tolerate PO.
whyWrong.B: Outpatient management ± selective antibiotics is standard per AGA 2023.
whyWrong.C: Surgery for Hinchey III/IV (abscess, perforation) or recurrent episodes.
whyWrong.D: Drainage for abscess >3cm (Hinchey Ib/II).
whyWrong.E: Colonoscopy after acute episode resolves (6-8 weeks) to rule out malignancy.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Uncomplicated diverticulitis: Outpatient management. Antibiotics are SELECTIVE (not mandatory). Colonoscopy in 6-8 weeks to rule out malignancy.

vignette: A 40-year-old HBeAg-positive chronic hepatitis B patient has HBV DNA 50,000 IU/mL and ALT 120 U/L. Liver biopsy shows moderate inflammation (Metavir A2F2). What is the BEST first-line treatment per AASLD 2024?

options.text: Tenofovir alafenamide (TAF) 25mg daily OR Entecavir 0.5mg daily

explanation.whyCorrect: Chronic HBV meeting treatment criteria: HBV DNA >2000 IU/mL + ALT >ULN + Moderate inflammation (A2F2). First-line: Tenofovir alafenamide (TAF) or Entecavir. TAF preferred over TDF (less renal and bone toxicity). Pegylated interferon is alternative for young patients with favorable genotype (HBeAg+, genotype A). Lamivudine and Adefovir are obsolete (high resistance).

whyWrong.A: Lamivudine has 70% resistance at 5 years (obsolete).
whyWrong.B: TAF or Entecavir: High barrier to resistance, excellent efficacy, safe.
whyWrong.C: Peg-IFN is alternative but more side effects; TAF/Entecavir preferred.
whyWrong.D: Adefovir is obsolete (nephrotoxic, weak potency).
whyWrong.E: Meets treatment criteria (DNA >2000 + elevated ALT + inflammation).

explanation.clinicalPearl: Chronic HBV treatment: TAF or Entecavir (first-line, high barrier to resistance). TAF > TDF (less bone/renal toxicity). Avoid lamivudine.

vignette: A 50-year-old treatment-naive male with HCV genotype 1a, no cirrhosis, and normal renal function is newly diagnosed. What is the recommended treatment per AASLD/IDSA 2024?

options.text: Glecaprevir/Pibrentasvir (Mavyret) x8 weeks OR Sofosbuvir/Velpatasvir (Epclusa) x12 weeks

explanation.whyCorrect: HCV treatment in 2024: Pan-genotypic DAA regimens. Glecaprevir/Pibrentasvir (Mavyret) x8 weeks or Sofosbuvir/Velpatasvir (Epclusa) x12 weeks. Both have >95% SVR (cure). Simplified algorithm: No genotype or fibrosis testing needed in non-cirrhotic patients. Interferon-based regimens are NO LONGER used. Treatment is curative and prevents progression to cirrhosis and HCC.

whyWrong.A: Interferon-based regimens are obsolete (low cure rate, severe side effects).
whyWrong.B: Pan-genotypic DAA x8-12 weeks = >95% cure. Simplified approach.
whyWrong.C: Ribavirin-containing regimens are no longer needed for most patients.
whyWrong.D: Curative therapies available now; no need to wait.
whyWrong.E: All HCV should be treated to prevent cirrhosis and HCC.

explanation.clinicalPearl: HCV cure in 2024: Glecaprevir/Pibrentasvir x8 weeks or Sofosbuvir/Velpatasvir x12 weeks. >95% SVR. No interferon. Simplified!

vignette: A 35-year-old female with IBS-C (constipation-predominant, Rome IV criteria) has failed fiber supplementation, osmotic laxatives, and lubiprostone. What is the BEST next therapy?

explanation.whyCorrect: IBS-C refractory to first-line: Tenapanor (NHE3 inhibitor) is FDA-approved for IBS-C. Reduces sodium absorption → increases intestinal fluid → accelerates transit. Also reduces visceral hypersensitivity. Alternative: Tegaserod (5-HT4 agonist, restricted access) or Plecanatide (guanylate cyclase-C agonist). Rifaximin is for IBS-D. Loperamide is for diarrhea (IBS-D).

whyWrong.A: Fiber may worsen bloating in IBS-C.
whyWrong.B: Tenapanor: New mechanism (NHE3 inhibitor), FDA-approved for IBS-C.
whyWrong.C: Loperamide is for IBS-D, not IBS-C.
whyWrong.D: Antispasmodics help abdominal pain but not constipation.
whyWrong.E: Rifaximin is for IBS-D, not IBS-C.

explanation.clinicalPearl: IBS-C refractory: Tenapanor (NHE3 inhibitor) or Plecanatide (GC-C agonist). IBS-D: Rifaximin or Eluxadoline.

vignette: A 55-year-old male with compensated alcoholic cirrhosis (Child-Pugh A) has small esophageal varices on screening EGD. He has never bled. What is the recommended management per AASLD 2024?

options.text: Non-selective beta-blocker (propranolol or carvedilol) for primary prophylaxis

explanation.whyCorrect: Small varices with high-risk features (Child-Pugh B/C or red wale signs): Non-selective beta-blocker (NSBB) for primary prophylaxis. Carvedilol 6.25-12.5mg daily is preferred (more effective than propranolol in reducing portal pressure). Small varices without high-risk features: NSBB optional (controversial). Medium/large varices: NSBB or EVL (equal efficacy). EVL is for medium/large varices or NSBB intolerance.

whyWrong.A: EVL is for medium/large varices or NSBB intolerance/contraindication.
whyWrong.B: NSBB (carvedilol preferred) for primary prophylaxis of small varices with high-risk features.
whyWrong.C: TIPS is for recurrent bleeding despite EVL + NSBB.
whyWrong.D: Small varices with risk factors warrant prophylaxis.
whyWrong.E: Sclerotherapy is obsolete (replaced by EVL).

explanation.clinicalPearl: Small varices + High risk: NSBB (carvedilol). Medium/large varices: NSBB or EVL. TIPS for refractory bleeding.

vignette: A 22-year-old female presents with acute liver failure (jaundice, coagulopathy, encephalopathy) 2 days after taking 15g of acetaminophen in a suicide attempt. Acetaminophen level is 50 μg/mL at 12 hours post-ingestion. What is the BEST management?

options.text: N-acetylcysteine (NAC) IV per protocol + Liver transplant evaluation

explanation.whyCorrect: Acetaminophen-induced ALF with detectable level and encephalopathy: NAC IV immediately (most effective if given within 8-10 hours but still beneficial later). King's College Criteria predict prognosis and need for transplant. Charcoal only if within 1-2 hours of ingestion. Already in ALF phase = NAC + Transplant evaluation. NAC reduces mortality even in established ALF.

whyWrong.A: Charcoal useful only within 1-2 hours of ingestion; already 12 hours.
whyWrong.B: NAC is antidote + Transplant evaluation for ALF.
whyWrong.C: ALF requires specific treatment (NAC) and transplant consideration.
whyWrong.D: Hemodialysis not effective for acetaminophen removal.
whyWrong.E: UDCA is for cholestatic liver disease, not ALF.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Acetaminophen ALF: NAC IV (even late). King's College Criteria predict transplant need. NAC reduces mortality.

vignette: A 25-year-old female with suspected celiac disease has tTG-IgA >10x ULN and positive endomysial antibodies. What is the recommended approach per ACG 2024?

options.text: Duodenal biopsy is mandatory for all patients
No-biopsy diagnosis is acceptable in symptomatic adults with tTG-IgA >10x ULN + positive EMA

explanation.whyCorrect: ACG 2024: No-biopsy diagnosis is acceptable in SYMPTOMATIC adults with tTG-IgA >10x ULN + positive endomysial antibodies (EMA) on a separate blood draw. Sensitivity and specificity approach 100% with this combination. Children can be diagnosed with tTG-IgA >10x ULN alone (ESPGHAN criteria). Biopsy still required if: Asymptomatic, tTG-IgA <10x ULN, negative EMA, or discordant results.

whyWrong.A: Biopsy no longer mandatory for all; no-biopsy criteria now accepted in adults.
whyWrong.B: No-biopsy diagnosis: tTG-IgA >10x ULN + positive EMA = Confirmed celiac disease.
whyWrong.C: Gluten-free diet should NOT be started before diagnostic testing (affects results).
whyWrong.D: HLA-DQ2/DQ8 has high NPV but low PPV; not diagnostic alone.
whyWrong.E: Treat now; repeat serology to monitor response to gluten-free diet.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Celiac diagnosis 2024: No-biopsy acceptable with tTG-IgA >10x ULN + EMA+. Children: tTG-IgA >10x ULN alone. Still need biopsy for most adults.

vignette: A 65-year-old female is found to have a 2.5cm branch-duct IPMN without high-risk features (no mural nodule, no dilated MPD, no jaundice). What is the surveillance per AGA 2024?

options.text: MRI/MRCP or EUS in 6-12 months, then consider extending interval if stable

explanation.whyCorrect: Branch-duct IPMN 2-3cm without worrisome features: Surveillance with MRI/MRCP (preferred) or EUS. Fukuoka guidelines (revised 2023): 2-3cm = Follow in 6-12 months. If stable at 1 year, extend interval to every 2 years. Surgery if: Mural nodule ≥5mm, MPD ≥10mm, jaundice, or rapid growth ≥5mm/2 years. Small IPMN <2cm: Follow in 1-2 years.

whyWrong.A: Surgery reserved for high-risk features or malignancy concern.
whyWrong.B: MRI/MRCP in 6-12 months for 2-3cm IPMN without high-risk features.
whyWrong.C: CT has radiation; MRI preferred for surveillance. Annual CT excessive.
whyWrong.D: IPMN requires surveillance (malignant potential).
whyWrong.E: ERCP with brushing is for high-risk features or suspected malignancy.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Branch-duct IPMN surveillance: 2-3cm = MRI in 6-12mo. <2cm = MRI in 1-2yr. Surgery if mural nodule ≥5mm, MPD ≥10mm, or growth.

vignette: A 50-year-old male with heartburn and regurgitation has failed twice-daily PPI therapy for 8 weeks. EGD is normal. What is the BEST next step per ACG 2024?

options.text: Ambulatory pH monitoring (Bravo or impedance-pH) off PPI to confirm acid reflux

explanation.whyCorrect: Refractory GERD symptoms despite BID PPI with normal EGD: Objective pH testing (ambulatory reflux monitoring). Bravo capsule (48-96h) or impedance-pH probe. Performed OFF PPI (after 7-day washout) to confirm/exclude pathologic acid reflux. If pH study negative: Consider functional heartburn (treat with neuromodulators). If positive: Consider surgery (fundoplication) or TIF (transoral incisionless fundoplication).

whyWrong.A: Increasing PPI beyond BID is rarely effective.
whyWrong.B: pH monitoring OFF PPI = Gold standard for refractory GERD workup.
whyWrong.C: Surgery only after confirmed pathologic reflux on pH testing.
whyWrong.D: Barium swallow evaluates anatomy (hernia, stricture) but not reflux.
whyWrong.E: H2 blockers provide minimal additional benefit with PPIs.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Refractory GERD + Normal EGD: pH monitoring OFF PPI (Bravo or impedance-pH). Confirms reflux vs functional heartburn.

vignette: A 55-year-old female with PBC has inadequate response to ursodeoxycholic acid (UDCA) after 12 months (ALP remains 2x ULN). What is the BEST add-on therapy per AASLD 2024?

explanation.whyCorrect: PBC with inadequate UDCA response (ALP >1.67x ULN after 12 months): Add Obeticholic acid (farnesoid X receptor agonist) - reduces ALP and improves surrogate markers. POISE trial showed significant ALP reduction. Bezafibrate (PPAR agonist) is alternative (off-label in US, approved in Europe). Both improve transplant-free survival. Liver transplant if decompensated or MELD >15.

whyWrong.A: Increasing UDCA beyond 13-15mg/kg provides no additional benefit.
whyWrong.B: OCA or Bezafibrate are second-line therapies for inadequate UDCA response.
whyWrong.C: Steroids have no role in PBC.
whyWrong.D: Azathioprine not effective in PBC.
whyWrong.E: Transplant evaluation reserved for decompensated cirrhosis or severe pruritus.

explanation.clinicalPearl: PBC inadequate UDCA response: Add OCA or Bezafibrate. ALP goal <1.67x ULN. Monitor for pruritus (OCA may worsen).

vignette: A 40-year-old female with AIH (elevated AST/ALT, IgG 2500, ANA/ASMA positive, biopsy showing interface hepatitis) is treatment-naive. What is the FIRST-LINE regimen per AASLD 2024?

explanation.whyCorrect: Treatment-naive AIH: Prednisone (or budesonide for non-cirrhotic) + Azathioprine. Prednisone 30-40mg daily taper + Azathioprine 50-100mg daily (started simultaneously or after TPMT testing). Budesonide 9mg daily can substitute prednisone in non-cirrhotic patients (fewer systemic side effects, equal efficacy). Mycophenolate is second-line for azathioprine-intolerant. Monotherapy with either agent is inferior.

whyWrong.A: Prednisone monotherapy has more side effects and lower remission rates.
whyWrong.B: Combination therapy = Standard of care. Allows lower steroid doses.
whyWrong.C: Azathioprine monotherapy inadequate for induction.
whyWrong.D: Mycophenolate is second-line (azathioprine-intolerant).
whyWrong.E: Budesonide is for non-cirrhotic AIH; azathioprine is added for maintenance.

explanation.clinicalPearl: AIH first-line: Prednisone + Azathioprine. Budesonide alternative in non-cirrhotic. Mycophenolate if intolerant to azathioprine.

vignette: A 65-year-old female has her third recurrence of C. difficile within 6 months despite appropriate treatment with vancomycin and fidaxomicin courses. What is the BEST treatment per IDSA 2024?

explanation.whyCorrect: Multiple recurrent C. diff (≥3 episodes): FMT is the treatment of choice. Restores healthy gut microbiome. >90% cure rate. FDA-approved RBX2660 (Rebyota - rectal microbiota) and SER-109 (Vowst - oral capsules) are now available as standardized microbiota-based therapies. These are PREFERRED over traditional stool-based FMT. Vancomycin taper is second-line if FMT unavailable.

whyWrong.A: Vancomycin taper is second-line; FMT preferred for multiple recurrences.
whyWrong.B: FMT (or FDA-approved microbiota therapies) = Standard for ≥3 recurrences.
whyWrong.C: Metronidazole is no longer recommended for C. diff treatment.
whyWrong.D: IV vancomycin does NOT reach colonic lumen.
whyWrong.E: Probiotics insufficient for established recurrent C. diff.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Multiple recurrent C. diff: FMT or FDA-approved microbiota therapies (Rebyota, Vowst). >90% cure. Vancomycin taper = alternative.

vignette: A 20-year-old male presents with tremor, dysarthria, Kayser-Fleischer rings on slit-lamp exam, and elevated liver enzymes. Ceruloplasmin is 8 mg/dL (low), 24h urine copper is 150 μg (elevated). What is the FIRST-LINE treatment?

explanation.whyCorrect: Symptomatic Wilson disease (neurologic + hepatic): D-penicillamine is first-line chelator. Promotes urinary copper excretion. Trientine is alternative (fewer side effects, increasingly used as first-line in Europe). Zinc is for MAINTENANCE after decoppering or asymptomatic/pre-symptomatic patients. Liver transplant for acute liver failure or decompensated cirrhosis. Treatment is lifelong.

whyWrong.A: D-penicillamine: First-line chelator for symptomatic Wilson disease.
whyWrong.B: Trientine: Alternative chelator (fewer side effects, preferred in some guidelines).
whyWrong.C: Zinc: Maintenance/pre-symptomatic; blocks copper absorption, slow onset.
whyWrong.D: Transplant for acute liver failure or decompensated cirrhosis unresponsive to chelation.
whyWrong.E: Untreated Wilson disease is fatal.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Wilson disease: D-penicillamine or Trientine for symptomatic (chelation). Zinc for maintenance. Lifelong treatment. Kayser-Fleischer rings = Pathognomonic.

vignette: A 60-year-old female presents with chronic watery diarrhea (non-bloody) for 3 months. Colonoscopy is normal. Biopsies show intraepithelial lymphocytosis and thickened collagen band (>15μm). What is the BEST treatment?

explanation.whyCorrect: Collagenous colitis (microscopic colitis): Budesonide 9mg daily x8 weeks is FIRST-LINE. Induces remission in 80% of patients. Taper after remission. Stop offending drugs (NSAIDs, PPIs, SSRIs). Loperamide for mild symptoms. Prednisone reserved for budesonide-refractory cases. Infliximab and surgery are last resorts.

whyWrong.A: Loperamide is for mild symptoms; budesonide is disease-modifying.
whyWrong.B: Budesonide (gut-targeted steroid) = First-line. 80% remission.
whyWrong.C: Systemic steroids reserved for budesonide-refractory cases.
whyWrong.D: Biologics for severe refractory disease (rarely needed).
whyWrong.E: Colectomy is last resort (extremely rare).

explanation.clinicalPearl: Microscopic colitis: Budesonide 9mg x8 weeks (first-line). Stop NSAIDs/PPIs/SSRIs. Collagenous > Lymphocytic.

vignette: A 45-year-old diabetic female with gastroparesis (confirmed by gastric emptying study) has nausea, vomiting, and early satiety despite dietary modification and metoclopramide. What is the BEST next therapy?

options.text: Gastric electrical stimulator (Enterra) OR G-POEM (per-oral endoscopic myotomy)

explanation.whyCorrect: Refractory gastroparesis failing medical therapy: Gastric electrical stimulator (Enterra) or G-POEM (gastric per-oral endoscopic pyloromyotomy). Enterra improves nausea/vomiting in 50-60% (does not improve gastric emptying). G-POEM is newer endoscopic option that divides pylorus to improve gastric emptying. Erythromycin causes tachyphylaxis (not for long-term).

whyWrong.A: Metoclopramide has black box warning (tardive dyskinesia); increasing dose worsens risk.
whyWrong.B: Enterra or G-POEM for refractory gastroparesis.
whyWrong.C: Erythromycin has tachyphylaxis within 2-4 weeks (not for chronic use).
whyWrong.D: Total gastrectomy is last resort (extreme cases).
whyWrong.E: Domperidone not FDA-approved (available via IND or internationally).

explanation.clinicalPearl: Refractory gastroparesis: Enterra (gastric stimulator) or G-POEM. Metoclopramide has black box warning. Erythromycin short-term only.

vignette: A 47-year-old African American male at average risk for CRC asks about screening initiation and options. What is the recommendation per USPSTF/ACS 2024?

options.text: Start screening at age 50 with colonoscopy
Start screening at age 45 with colonoscopy or FIT
Start screening at age 40 with flexible sigmoidoscopy
Start screening at age 55 with CT colonography
No screening needed until symptoms develop

explanation.whyCorrect: USPSTF 2021 (Grade B) and ACS 2023: CRC screening begins at AGE 45 for ALL average-risk adults (African Americans included but no longer require earlier screening). Options: Colonoscopy q10yr (gold standard), FIT annually (most common non-invasive), MT-sDNA (Cologuard) q3yr, CT colonography q5yr, Flex sig q5yr. Positive non-invasive tests require follow-up colonoscopy.

whyWrong.A: Age 45, not 50 (changed in 2021).
whyWrong.B: Age 45 for all average-risk adults. Colonoscopy or FIT are primary options.
whyWrong.C: Age 45, not 40. Flex sig alone is less preferred.
whyWrong.D: Age 45, not 55. CT colonography is one option.
whyWrong.E: Screening is recommended BEFORE symptoms develop.

explanation.clinicalPearl: CRC screening starts at AGE 45 for all average-risk adults. Colonoscopy q10yr or FIT annually. African Americans start at 45 (same as all).

vignette: A 25-year-old Asian female presents with bloating, flatulence, and watery diarrhea after consuming milk and ice cream. Symptoms resolve with dairy avoidance. What is the BEST diagnostic test?

options.text: Clinical history and dietary elimination trial

explanation.whyCorrect: Lactose intolerance: Clinical history + response to dietary elimination is sufficient for diagnosis. Lactose breath hydrogen test is confirmatory if needed. Small bowel biopsy measures lactase activity (research, not clinical). Serum lactase not useful. Trial of lactose-free diet with symptom resolution = Diagnostic. Lactase enzyme supplements with dairy = Treatment.

whyWrong.A: Breath test is confirmatory but clinical diagnosis is usually sufficient.
whyWrong.B: Biopsy is invasive; not routine for lactose intolerance.
whyWrong.C: Serum lactase not clinically useful.
whyWrong.D: Clinical history + dietary elimination trial = Practical and sufficient.
whyWrong.E: Stool reducing substances used in infants.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Lactose intolerance: Clinical diagnosis (history + dietary response). Breath test if needed. Lactase supplements = Treatment.

vignette: A 50-year-old male with fatigue, arthralgias, and elevated ferritin (1200 ng/mL) and transferrin saturation 60%. HFE gene shows C282Y homozygosity. ALT is 80. What is the BEST treatment?

explanation.whyCorrect: Hereditary hemochromatosis (C282Y/C282Y) with iron overload: Therapeutic phlebotomy is the cornerstone. Remove 500mL (250mg iron) 1-2x weekly until ferritin <50-100 ng/mL and transferrin saturation <50%. Then maintenance phlebotomy every 2-4 months. Dietary iron restriction is adjunct. Iron chelation reserved for those who cannot tolerate phlebotomy (anemia).

whyWrong.A: Chelation is second-line for patients who cannot undergo phlebotomy.
whyWrong.B: Phlebotomy = Simple, effective, first-line. Remove iron stores.
whyWrong.C: Dietary restriction alone insufficient for iron overload.
whyWrong.D: Erythropoietin is for anemia of chronic disease, not hemochromatosis.
whyWrong.E: Ferritin 1200 with liver inflammation requires treatment.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Hemochromatosis: Phlebotomy weekly until ferritin <50-100. Then maintenance q2-4mo. C282Y homozygosity = Most common.

vignette: A 75-year-old female with atrial fibrillation (not on anticoagulation) presents with severe abdominal pain out of proportion to exam, metabolic acidosis, and elevated lactate. What is the BEST next step?

explanation.whyCorrect: Acute mesenteric ischemia (AFib = embolic source): CT ANGIOGRAPHY is the diagnostic test of choice. Shows: Arterial occlusion (SMA embolus), bowel wall thickening, pneumatosis (late finding). Pain out of proportion + metabolic acidosis = Mesenteric ischemia until proven otherwise. Treatment: Emergent surgical consultation, revascularization, resection of necrotic bowel. Heparin alone insufficient.

whyWrong.A: US cannot adequately evaluate mesenteric vasculature.
whyWrong.B: CT angiography = Gold standard for mesenteric ischemia.
whyWrong.C: Surgery after CT confirms diagnosis and guides approach.
whyWrong.D: Heparin alone will not salvage ischemic bowel; need revascularization.
whyWrong.E: EGD evaluates stomach/duodenum, not small bowel ischemia.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Mesenteric ischemia: Pain out of proportion + Acidosis + AFib = CT angiography STAT. Call surgery. Mortality 50-70% if delayed.
